judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all five expectations with essentially equivalent citations and quotes; Report 2 additionally catches that the remediation description (isolation/traffic reroute) doesn't match the timeline's actual config rollback and omits outage duration, giving it a slight edge in thoroughness.

report_1.evidence: failures began at 14:03 and the first alert fired at 14:50 — a 47-minute detection gap

report_2.evidence: failures began at 14:03; the FIRST ALERT fired at 14:50 — a 47-minute detection gap, not "within minutes."

report_1.evidence: incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md states 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing, and 37 were unrecoverable and refunded

report_2.evidence: incidents/OPS-412-timeline.md states: "During the window 1,284 transactions failed and required manual reprocessing; 37 were unrecoverable and refunded."

report_1.evidence: April uptime was 99.77% (metrics/uptime-2026.csv), below the 99.9% monthly SLA. docs/sla-commitments.md states any month below 99.9% "is a breach and owes service credits per the customer agreement (credits scale with the depth of the breach)."

report_2.evidence: docs/sla-commitments.md: the contractual monthly SLA is 99.9%, "Any calendar month below 99.9% is a breach and owes service credits." metrics/uptime-2026.csv shows 2026-04,99.77 — 99.77%, below 99.9%. April was a contractual breach that owes service credits.

report_1.evidence: The line "caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change" assigns blame to a person. docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." Rewrite as a process/system cause.

report_2.evidence: Draft line: "The outage was caused by the on-call engineer deploying an untested config change." docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md rule 1: "Never assign blame to an individual." This must be rewritten to describe the cause without pointing at a person.

report_1.evidence: It leads with marketing, not impact — violates style-guide rule 3. The draft opens with a 99.99% uptime boast and reliability self-praise; rule 3 requires leading with customer impact and remediation. Restructure to open with what happened to customers and what LarkPay is doing about it.

report_2.evidence: Does not lead with customer impact — violates style guide rule 3. The draft opens with an uptime boast ("99.99% uptime… commitment to reliability"). docs/customer-comms-style-guide.md rule 3: "Lead with customer impact and remediation." The document must be reordered to lead with what happened to customers and what is being done, not with reputation.
